subject: [PATCH v2] fs/proc/task_mmu: fix PAGEMAP_SCAN written state for PMD holes

reviews.author: Andrew Morton
reviews.summary:
The reviewer asked Kiryl to explain how the patch will address the issue from the user's perspective, specifically what visible effects the bug has on userspace and how the patch fixes these issues.

The reviewer questioned the use of custom alignment macros in the selftest code, suggesting that standard Linux kernel macros should be used instead.
reviews.raw_body:
OK ;)

But what do our users see?  afaict the result of the bug is "the next
incremental CRIU dump keeps stale data".  Why is this a problem?  How
would operators look at a user bug report and figure out that this
patch will address it?  Is there some Reported-by/Closes?

In other words, (please train Claude to) always describe the userspace
visible effects of a bug when fixing it.

---

Do selftests not have ALIGN and friends?  Seems not, given how many of
them have own implementations.

2026-07-08.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the PAGEMAP_SCAN functionality of the Linux kernel, specifically when dealing with PMD holes (large page table entries). The issue arises when an anonymous huge page is write-protected and then cleared using MADV_DONTNEED. As a result, the range is not reported as written, leading to stale data in subsequent incremental dumps. To fix this, the patch adds the PAGE_IS_WRITTEN category to the evaluation of holes in non-hugetlb uffd-wp VMAs, ensuring that PMD holes are correctly reported as written. A selftest has also been added to verify the fix.
